Weight statistics across training — Pythia

Reproduction of beren, "Basic facts about language models during training" on Pythia checkpoints.

Histogram of weight values — one translucent curve per layer of the selected type, overlaid and animated over training (press play or drag the slider). Gaussian at init, sharpening into a peaky, heavy-tailed (logistic-like) shape (beren's weight-distribution finding). Bins are fixed per type from the trained spread.

Symmetric α-stable stability index of each weight matrix's entry distribution (McCulloch quantile fit). α = 2 is Gaussian; α < 2 is heavy-tailed (power-law). One line per matrix (click the legend to isolate); the dashed line is α = 2. This is the entry-distribution index — distinct from the HT-SR α of the WᵀW spectrum — and it assumes stability, so read it next to the histogram shape (a Laplace/logistic with merely exponential tails also maps below 2).

Eigenvalues of X = (1/N) WᵀW (the squared singular values divided by N = the larger matrix dimension — Martin & Mahoney's normalization, so types are comparable) — the empirical spectral density (ESD) — histogrammed at each checkpoint and animated over training (press play / drag the slider), one translucent curve per layer of the selected type (same grouping as the weight histograms). At init it's a Marchenko–Pastur bulk; over training a few eigenvalues run out into a heavy tail. Each layer's histogram is normalized to area 1 (the density ρ_emp(λ)).

HT-SR α (Martin & Mahoney) is the power-law exponent of the tail of the WᵀW eigenvalue distribution (the ESD): ρ(λ) ∝ λ−α, fit to the eigenvalues λ = ν² (squared singular values) by the Clauset–Shalizi–Newman MLE — the fit our analysis has always computed. Smaller α = a heavier spectral tail = more strongly correlated / more "self-regularized"; well-trained layers cluster in α ∈ [2, 4] (the dashed guides at 2 and 4). At initialisation the spectrum is Marchenko–Pastur with no power-law tail, so α is large / ill-defined there (off the top of the axis).

This is a different α from the "α-stable index" in the Weights tab. That one is fit to the weight values — the entries Wij, a Lévy stability index in (0, 2]. This one is fit to the eigenvalue spectrum of WᵀW. They are linked only through a model (αHT-SR ≈ 1 + μ/2, if the entries were i.i.d. heavy-tailed with index μ), and empirically they move on different schedules: the spectrum goes heavy-tailed early (~step 1k) while the entries stay Gaussian until late (~step 20k) — because the spectral tail is driven by correlations in W, not by the heaviness of the individual weights. One line per layer of the selected type; α is huge / off-axis at init (random MP, no power law).
